Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The Results name the active transcript as Calam.02G160400.1 and Data Availability lists OM256537 for C. americana aucubin synthase.

Source_Species.reason: The enzyme was discovered from Callicarpa americana.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper explicitly uses the Latin name Callicarpa americana.

Applied_Species.reason: The tested enzyme candidate was from C. americana.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The enzyme was renamed Callicarpa americana aucubin synthase (CaAS).

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The candidate belongs to an orthogroup with UniProt annotations in the CYP72 family, within cytochrome P450s.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The study concerns iridoid glucoside biosynthesis, and CaAS catalyzes an aucubin-forming step in the epi-iridoid pathway.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper links the bartsioside→aucubin step to the ancestral aucubin/catalpol route and compares it to the Damtoft-Jensen route II hypothesis.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results explicitly state Calam.02G160400.1 produced aucubin when incubated with bartsioside.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Bartsioside was the tested substrate giving product.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The direct enzymatic product reported is aucubin.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Aucubin is discussed in the context of iridoid glucosides.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: CaAS directly converts substrate to the target product aucubin.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Within the predicted pathway, bartsioside is upstream of aucubin, so this is a downstream oxidation step in the aucubin branch.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not establish CaAS as rate-limiting or a control step.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The core experimental finding is production of aucubin from bartsioside in microsome assays, identifying the predicted aucubin-forming enzyme.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states iridoids act on generalist herbivores, supporting a defense-related function for this metabolite class.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: C. americana candidates were selected by coexpression with early iridoid genes using transcriptomes from mature and young leaves, stems, petioles, roots, closed buds, open flowers, and fruit.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The candidate was selected from the coexpressed cluster containing early iridoid biosynthetic genes in C. americana.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The enzyme was assayed in yeast microsomes, but the native subcellular localization of CaAS in plant cells was not established.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state candidate genes were cloned into the Gal10 MCS of pESC-Leu2d for yeast expression.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed elements are described: coexpression-based selection, orthogroup/PFAM filtering, heterologous yeast microsomes, substrate incubation, and LC-MS/XIC detection.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Methods specify in vitro microsome assays with NADPH, overnight at 30 °C in the dark.

Omics_Data.reason: The paper says C. americana transcriptome/expression data came from published data (Hamilton et al. 2020) and were used with orthogroup analysis; no specific transcriptome accession is given here.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper says pathway hypotheses facilitate enzyme discovery and pathway assembly, but does not explicitly claim a breeding/engineering application for CaAS in those terms.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: For heterologous expression the construct contained a cytochrome P450 reductase from Artemisia annua, a required assay partner.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is directly supported: identification from a CYP72 orthogroup among coexpressed candidates, conversion of bartsioside to aucubin, and validation of a predicted step absent from prior Callicarpa reports.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper states it was renamed CaAS and notes OG0001031 contained four enzymes, three in C. americana, with CYP72 annotations.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results cite VIAG_c41279_g1_i4 and Data Availability lists OM256538 for V. agnus-castus aucubin synthase.

Source_Species.reason: The orthologue tested was from Vitex agnus-castus.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Explicitly stated as V. agnus-castus / Vitex agnus-castus.

Applied_Species.reason: The tested orthologue was from V. agnus-castus.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 11 states candidates were renamed as aucubin synthase for each species, including VaAS.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: VaAS is described as an orthologue from the same clade as CaAS, but the paper does not explicitly restate PFAM/UniProt CYP72 annotation for this exact transcript in the main text.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The orthologue catalyzes the aucubin-forming step in iridoid glucoside biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The reaction is discussed as part of the predicted ancestral aucubin/catalpol route.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results and Fig. 11 show the orthologue catalyzed the predicted reaction from bartsioside to aucubin.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Bartsioside was the substrate used in microsome incubations.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Aucubin is an iridoid glucoside in the paper's framework.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The enzyme directly forms aucubin from bartsioside.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: This is the oxidation step from bartsioside to aucubin in the aucubin branch.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: No rate-limiting role was established in the paper.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The ortholog catalyzed the predicted bartsioside-to-aucubin conversion.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper attributes defensive roles against generalist herbivores to iridoids generally.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Only microsomal assay context is shown; native localization in V. agnus-castus is not determined.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Candidate genes were expressed from the Gal10 MCS in yeast; this applies to tested candidates including VaAS.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Ortholog selection, yeast microsomal expression, substrate feeding, and LC-MS/XIC detection are all described.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Microsome incubations with NADPH at 30 °C in the dark are described in Methods.

Omics_Data.reason: The transcript ID is provided and the study used transcriptome/orthogroup analysis; GenBank accession OM256538 is listed.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This translational application is not explicitly stated for VaAS.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The heterologous expression vector contained Artemisia annua CPR for P450 assays.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The paper supports selection of a V. agnus-castus orthologue from the same clade, its bartsioside-to-aucubin activity, and its support for ancestral pathway conservation.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The enzyme was named VaAS, and the rationale for testing included that Vitex is the genus containing the only bartsioside report in the curated data set.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results cite PATO_c46653_g6_i1 and Data Availability lists OM256539 for P. tomentosa aucubin synthase.

Source_Species.reason: The tested orthologue was from Paulownia tomentosa.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Explicitly stated as P. tomentosa / Paulownia tomentosa.

Applied_Species.reason: The tested enzyme was from P. tomentosa.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 11 states the candidate was renamed PtAS, an aucubin synthase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: PtAS is said to be an orthologue from the same clade, but the paper does not explicitly annotate this exact transcript as CYP72 in the Results text.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The orthologue catalyzes the aucubin-forming step within iridoid glucoside biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The reaction is discussed as part of the ancestral aucubin/catalpol route.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results and Fig. 11 show the orthologue catalyzed the predicted reaction from bartsioside to aucubin.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The direct reaction product is aucubin.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Aucubin is an iridoid glucoside in this study.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The enzyme directly forms the target product aucubin.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The reaction is the oxidation of bartsioside to aucubin in the aucubin branch.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: No rate-limiting or control role is claimed.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The outgroup ortholog catalyzed the predicted bartsioside-to-aucubin conversion.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Iridoids are described as acting on generalist herbivores, supporting a defense-related role for the class.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Only assay in microsomes is shown; native localization is not demonstrated.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The expression system used the Gal10 MCS in pESC-Leu2d for candidate genes.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Ortholog selection, yeast microsomal expression, substrate feeding, and LC-MS/XIC product detection are all described.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Methods specify NADPH-dependent in vitro microsome incubation at 30 °C in the dark.

Omics_Data.reason: The transcript ID is given and the work used transcriptome/orthogroup analysis; GenBank accession OM256539 is listed.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Comparative/engineering utility is a plausible implication but not explicitly claimed.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The heterologous assay system coexpressed Artemisia annua CPR with candidate P450s.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The paper supports all three points: outgroup ortholog selection, bartsioside-to-aucubin conversion, and reinforcement of ancestral-pathway inference.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper names it PtAS and explicitly says the outgroup validation strengthened the hypothesis that the reaction was present in the ancestral pathway.
